items.title: French-Iranian director and author Marjane Satrapi dead at 56
items.summary_auto: Acclaimed Iranian-French cartoonist and filmmaker Marjane Satrapi, a prominent advocate for women's rights, has died at 56. French President Emmanuel Macron is among those paying tribute to the artist.

items.title: Missing Everest guide found alive crawling to base camp after family had started funeral arrangements
items.summary_auto: A Sherpa guide was found crawling to base camp on Mount Everest a week after he went missing and was reunited with his family after they'd given up hope he would return.

items.title: Missing Mount Everest guide Dawa Sherpa found alive after crawling back to base camp
items.summary_auto: A Sherpa guide was found crawling to base camp on Mount Everest a week after he went missing and was reunited with his family after they'd given up hope he would return.

items.title: Israel strikes Lebanon as U.S.-brokered ceasefire yet to take hold, according to Lebanese president
items.summary_auto: Lebanon's president has said ‌a U.S.-brokered ceasefire that the country and Israel agreed to the previous evening could come into force within ​24 hours, according to local media, but militant group Hezbollah has not signed on as Israel's military vows to continue striking the south of Lebanon.

items.title: Trump says he's nominating Todd Blanche to serve as U.S. attorney general
items.summary_auto: U.S. President Donald Trump said Wednesday that he will nominate  Todd Blanche to serve as attorney general, tapping his former personal lawyer who has aggressively pursued the Republican president's agenda while leading the Justice Department in an acting role

items.title: Will the Alberta-Canada carbon pricing deal work? A new analysis is skeptical
items.summary_auto: A new analysis shows the Carney government's pipeline deal could lead Canada further from its climate targets, even with an agreement on carbon pricing.

items.title: N.S. removes short story containing N-word from online high school course
items.summary_auto: Nova Scotia’s Education Department has removed a short story from an online Grade 12 English course after a parent raised concerns and CBC News inquired about the text's repeated use of a racist term for Black people.

items.title: 1 year after acquittal in Hockey Canada sexual assault trial, Carter Hart could win Stanley Cup
items.summary_auto: As the Vegas Golden Knights advance in the Stanley Cup Final, at the centre of their championship run is Canadian goaltender Carter Hart — whose journey has been highly successful on ice and deeply controversial off it.

items.title: Hollywood's latest fixation? The young YouTubers behind box-office hits like Backrooms and Obsession
items.summary_auto: Summer movie season is normally a battle of Hollywood heavyweights — but this year, two of the hottest tickets at the box office come from young YouTubers who brought their built-in audiences with them to the big screen. Industry watchers say their success could influence what projects get made in the future.

items.title: Her husband is accused in an AI deepfake investigation. She’s filing for divorce
items.summary_auto: The wife of a man accused in an AI deepfake investigation is speaking out to express support for the alleged victims, to call for stronger laws to deal with the kind of behaviour of which her husband stands accused, and to start a conversation about support available for people in her position, who are not formally victims of alleged crimes, but remain deeply affected.

items.title: Supersized data centres are coming to Canada. One province is at the epicentre
items.summary_auto: A new national AI strategy by the federal government this week comes at a time when the country is confronting a wave of new high-powered data centres, while public sentiment could be souring on the impacts of the new technology.

items.title: Higher oil and gas prices coming soon, industry and analysts warn
items.summary_auto: Energy executives and analysts are warning that oil and gas prices will likely rise significantly as the Strait of Hormuz remains closed and stubborn demand burns through strategic reserves.

items.title: Jet fuel shortage? Don't worry about it, say major airlines
items.summary_auto: More than six weeks after international warnings that Europe could run out of jet fuel, at least two major global carriers are saying it's safe to book a summer holiday to Europe because they don't expect the expensive commodity to run out during the busy travel season.

items.title: #TheMoment the biggest ever Canadian flag on a mountaintop was unfurled
items.summary_auto: Grouse Mountain's Adam Rootman tells The National about the moment his team broke the world record for 'largest flag unfurled on a mountaintop' on the ski hill in North Vancouver, B.C., as neighbouring Vancouver prepares for the FIFA World Cup.

items.title: Canada's forestry crisis should be blamed on homegrown problems, not Trump, federal report says
items.summary_auto: The report says obstacles include unstable access to affordable fibre, excessive regulations, persistent underinvestment in manufacturing, weak capacity to innovate, and inadequate domestic demand for wood-based products.

items.title: Emaciated grey whales washing up on B.C. beaches 'canary in coal mine,' researcher warns
items.summary_auto: Two more grey whales have washed up in B.C., with researchers and experts both warning the province is on pace for a record breaking year.

items.title: Tornado warnings again pop up around southern Manitoba
items.summary_auto: People in southern Manitoba were subjected to another evening of tornado warnings on Wednesday, one day after three twisters touched down in the region.

items.title: B.C. charity scraps World Cup ticket raffle after letter from FIFA lawyers
items.summary_auto: A B.C. non-profit that supports people with spinal injuries was forced to cancel a raffle for a pair of World Cup tickets after receiving a nine-page letter from FIFA's lawyers, accusing it of trademark infringement and ticket rule violations.

items.title: ‘I heard it before I saw it’: Witness watches rockfall at Whistler Peak
items.summary_auto: The area where the rockfall occurred, at Whistler Peak on Whistler Blackcomb, was closed and no one was injured.

items.title: Government to begin paying out one-time GST credit top-up for eligible Canadians
items.summary_auto: The Liberal government's one-time GST rebate top-up, announced earlier this year, is set to begin landing in some Canadians' banks accounts starting on Friday.

items.title: Cuba's battered tourism sector takes another hit as Spain's Meliá hotel chain shutters some locations
items.summary_auto: Spanish hotel chain Meliá has joined a growing list of companies with a long-standing presence in Cuba that are withdrawing or limiting their operations on the island after the U.S. announced new sanctions while upholding an oil embargo.

items.title: A renowned Indigenous activist has died in Nicaraguan detention. His loved ones want answers
items.summary_auto: For three years after Nicaraguan Indigenous leader Brooklyn Rivera was arrested, his friends and family were kept in the dark about his whereabouts and wellbeing. Then, on Sunday, they learned he has died.

items.title: Want the Heated Rivalry Team Canada fleece? Well, it's already sold out
items.summary_auto: Miss out snagging the Shane Hollander Team Canada fleece? You aren't alone. Within 10 minutes of its Tuesday 12 p.m. launch, the hotly anticipated fleece completely sold out, according to Canadian apparel brand Province of Canada.

items.title: A woman died after wandering from home 12 times. N.B. seniors advocate says many 'saw it coming'
items.summary_auto: A woman with worsening dementia was left in a home for a year after Social Development found it would not meet her safety needs, New Brunswick's seniors advocate says.

items.title: Fighting a cold? A meal gives your immune cells an immediate boost
items.summary_auto: Experiments in humans and mice suggest that certain white blood cells, known as T-cells, may respond to infections more effectively after a meal. The research calls into question the notion of "starve a fever, feed a cold."

items.title: Albertans most likely in Canada to get financial advice from AI, social media, poll shows
items.summary_auto: Survey suggests 21 per cent of surveyed respondents in Alberta used models like ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude to get financial guidance. Experts say online sources are a good learning tool but warn they can have risks.

items.title: Spicy romance novel adaptation to be filmed in P.E.I. with $5M from province
items.summary_auto: The largest production ever to come to Prince Edward Island will begin this summer.

items.title: How Britain’s far right hijacked the murder of Henry Nowak
items.summary_auto: The brutal murder of 18-year-old student Henry Nowak in southern England has ignited another volatile debate in the U.K. over race and what’s been described as “white grievance.” Despite pleas from Nowak's grieving family to prevent his death from sparking racial division, prominent far-right and populist figures have tried to exploit the tragedy.

items.title: Mary Simon says serving as Governor General was the 'honour of a lifetime'
items.summary_auto: It was the "honour of a lifetime" to serve as the representative of the Crown in Canada for the past five years, outgoing Gov. Gen. Mary Simon said in a speech to parliamentarians on Wednesday.

items.title: Owners ordered to pay $100K in wages say they didn't know foreign workers were employed at their hotel
items.summary_auto: The owners of a hotel chain who are appealing orders to pay more than $100,000 in owed wages to three foreign workers say they weren't even aware two of the workers were employed at one of the company's Manitoba hotels.

items.title: Alberta man charged after more than 500,000 child sexual exploitation photos, videos seized
items.summary_auto: A man from Okotoks, Alta., faces criminal charges after police say they found more than half a million child sexual exploitation photos and videos in his possession. It's one of the largest collections the Alberta child exploitation unit has ever encountered.

items.title: Alternate captain Renata Fast re-signs with Toronto Sceptres
items.summary_auto: PWHL teams could each protect three players from signing with expansion teams. New teams in Hamilton, Detroit, Las Vegas and San Jose will debut next season.

items.title: Governor General-designate Louise Arbour meets King Charles ahead of swearing-in
items.summary_auto: King Charles received Louise Arbour at Buckingham Palace in London on Wednesday, before the former justice of the Supreme Court of Canada is sworn in as Governor General next week.

items.title: Nostalgia alert: MusiquePlus is relaunching on TikTok
items.summary_auto: Long before your feed told you what to listen to, Quebec culture had a different tastemaker: MusiquePlus. Well, music fans can rejoice now that the TV channel that helped launch the careers of many Quebec stars is being revived as a new kind of influencer on TikTok.

items.title: 4 migrant farm workers in Italy burned to death in car, according to survivor, police
items.summary_auto: Four migrants working as fruit pickers in slave-like conditions in southern Italy have been burned to death. The murder case is prompting fresh national soul searching over the exploitation of farm workers.

items.title: 'Anything is possible': Alphonso Davies refuses to rule himself out of Canada's World Cup opener
items.summary_auto: Canada captain Alphonso Davies trained on his own Wednesday as the men's national team continued preparations for the FIFA World Cup.

items.title: Ukrainian drones hit St. Petersburg oil terminal before city hosts Russian economic forum
items.summary_auto: Ukrainian long-range drones struck an oil terminal in St. Petersburg and set it ablaze, President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said Wednesday, sending smoke billowing over the city where President Vladimir Putin was born as it hosts Russia's leading event for attracting foreign capital.

items.title: South Park creators remain defiant, promise more Trump takedowns in new season
items.summary_auto: South Park creators Matt Stone and Trey Parker say they aren’t backing down from satirizing U.S. President Trump in the show’s upcoming season — even if, as they say, the latest group trying to censor them now “has a military.”
